title: 第 10 節
en: o 4 CHARACTER OF THE SMRITI.
Brahmans made of this argument, in order to substantiate the authority of their legal text-books.

" Does it not seem after all," he says, " as if this Smriti (containing as it does the laws of men) hardly deserved a commentary of its own, inasmuch as it is difficult to make out on what grounds it claims any authority ? For if we appeal to a Sutra of Jaimini's (the founder of the Purva-miinansa) where he has proved that the Veda possesses an authority irrespective of anything else, these arguments can hardly apply to books which are evidently the work of men, and entirely dependent on the authority of their sources. These sources again, if they be considered as the life and strength of that authority, are often very indistinct. First, they could never fall under the cognizance of the senses, because the very nature of duty or law is transcendental. Nor can this ultimate reason or source be found in induction, inasmuch as induction is only possible after observation. Neither can it be looked for in the sayings of other men, because man is exposed to error, and cannot even express things as he has really perceived them. But even if man was free from error, there would always be room for doubt and opposition. And as to finding the authority for these laws in direct precepts of the Sruti (Mantras and Brahmanas) this is out of the question, because such precepts are not to be found there. We have never seen a passage in the Veda where precepts like those of the Smriti, to keep the body clean, &c, are given. To admit the right of induction for laws of this kind would be most dangerous, for it would apply with equal force to the precepts of Buddha, to worship the holy sepulchre, &c. a However, there is an answer to all these doubts.

CHARACTER OF THE SMRITI. 89 A great difference exists between the Smritis of Manu and the Smritis of Buddha, because Manu's authority is asserted by the undeniable Veda itself. It is said in the Veda, 'Whatever Manu said, was medicine ;' but there is no passage there in any way favourable to the Smriti of Buddha, and therefore the right of applying induction cannot be considered dangerous, because it never could be extended to Buddha's doctrines.

11 Quod non," says the opponent. " This passage of the Veda, ' Whatever Manu said, was medicine/ is only an Arthavada (an explanatory remark), and has no evidence by itself. It only serves to illustrate or recommend another precept, viz., that two verses of Manu's are to be used at a certain sacrifice.1 Therefore, there is no passage in the Veda to warrant the authority of the Smriti ; and if £&kya's, i. e. Buddha's, Smriti be exceptionable, the same applies to the Smriti of Manu. Thus it is said, ' As men speak often untruth and are exposed to error, as no divine precept is given, faith only can be authority.' But 1 As dhayyas at the Somaraudra Cham, in the middle of the Samidheni, or fire-kindling hymns. The same argument occurs in Kumarila's Tantra-varttika, i. 3., ff^t ^ *mY^: ^sT^ra^rr *re?ftf^ fa^iraq^ wr *t«pr *rf^f^ c^TiSr^ irc^mT^rr Tt^rn Mahadeva, in his Commentary on the Hiranyakesi-sutras, says that the 6ruti bears witness to the authority of the Smritis by declaring that whatever Manu said was medicine.

further, even admitting that there was a shadow of authority for Manu, what could be said in favour of Parasara's Smriti, which is now to be explained ? For, although the Yeda may praise Manu, it never does the same for Parasara, and thus Parasara's authority at least can hardly be defended.

" Against all this our answer is : the Smritis are an authority, because that they should have authority is understood by itself ; and there is no reason why they should not be considered as having authority. Three reasons are given why Manu and the rest could not claim any authority, viz. i that men speak untruth, that they are exposed to error, and that no divine precept is given.' These objections, however, are entirely out of order, because nobody would ever think that Manu and Parasara, who have been perfect from their very birth, could have spoken untruth, and could have erred. So much for the first two objections. And who ever denied that these sages were perfect from their very birth, as Mantras, Arthavadas, Itihasas, and Puranas, prove distinctly not only the existence of Parasara and others, but also their perfection ? Nay, even if we had not the testimony of the Mantras, how could the perfection of Parasara and others be denied, which is involved in their very existence ? A difference of opinion is quite impossible. And has it not been proved in the chapter on the gods l in the Uttara-mimansa, that the Mantras do not require 1 If this refers to the Sankarshanakanda, which is ascribed to Jaimini, and forms an appendix to the Karmamimansa-sutras (cf. Prasthanabheda), we ought to read Purva-mimansa instead of Uttara-mimansa.

CHARACTER OF THE SMRITI. 91 any further proof for what they say ? It is true that in the chapter on the Arthavadas it has been admitted that what the Arthavadas contain is not always to be believed. But this is only on account of some impossible things which are occasionally mentioned therein. Therefore an Arthavada like this, ' Whatever Manu says is medicine/ although it only serves to recommend another rule, must yet be considered as true in itself. With regard to Sakya, nothing similar can be brought forward ; and thus it is well said elsewhere, l May a man scorn all the erroneous doctrines of Arhat, Ch&rvaka, and Buddha.' As regards Parasara in particular, it is wrong to say that his fame is not equally founded on the Yeda, for it is said in the Sruti, ! Thus spoke Vyasa, the son of Parasara/ If, therefore, the famous Veda-Vyasa is praised as the son of Parasara, how much more famous must not Parasara, his father, have been! In the genealogical Brahmana of the Vajasaneyi-sakha, the son also and the grandson of Parasara are mentioned in the succession of pupils and teachers who handed down the Yeda1, ' Ghritakausika received from Parasaryayana, Parasaryayana from Parasarya, P&rasarya from Jatukarnya, &c.' Therefore Par&sara stands quite on a level with Manu ; and the same applies to all the other Rishis, like Vasishtha and Yajnavalkya, who are authors of Smritis, and are mentioned in the Sruti. Thus we read, ' The Rishis did not see Indra clearly, but Vasishtha saw him clearly.' 2 1 Brihadaranyaka, 5. 6. 3.
2 Taittiriya-Sanhita, 3. 5. 2.
4 Atri gave his children to Aurva, who longed for a son.7 * l Yajnavalkya had two wives/ 2 Therefore one must not think of attacking the Smritis of Manu and others by any means.

" The third reason also which was brought forward against the authority of the Smriti, viz. that the f The Rishis did not see Indra clearly, but Vasishtha saw him clearly. Indra said, * I shall tell you a Brahmana, so that all men that are born will have thee for Purohita ; but do not tell of me to the other Rishis.' Thus he told him these parts of the hymns ; and ever since, men were born having Vasishtha for their Purohita. Therefore a Vasishtha is to be chosen as Brahman." Cf. Tandya Brahmana, xv. 5., where it is said of the Bharalas that they will always have a Vasishtha as Purohita. The Commentator there observes, that Bharata may either mean the kings of that name, or men in general.
1 Taittiriya-Sanhita, 7. 1. 8.

f^H: farfwr *rid^mi * tjct ^n^ro^TwrW^trn ^^TfTT ^*Hb ^*5fcr: II " Atri gave his children to the son of Urva, who longed for a son. Then he felt lonely, and saw that he was without power, weak, and decrepit. He saw this Chatxiratra ; he took it and sacrificed with it. Four sons were born to him from it, — a good Hotri, a good Udgatri, a good Adhvaryu, and a good Brahman."
2 6atapatha-brahmana, xvii. 4. 5.

CHARACTER OF THE SMRITI. 93 precepts given there are not based upon passages of the Sruti, does not hold good, because passages are met with which are the source of all the laws given in the Smriti. Thus we read, ' These five great sacrifices are every day commenced and every day performed: the Devayajna (to the gods), the Pitriyajna (to the fathers, the manes), the Bhutayajna (to all beings), the Manushyayajna (to men), the Brahmayajna (to Brahman, the divine Self)/ l And although there is no distinct precept in the Veda for ablutions, &c, yet all this is implied. Thus the Bhattaoharyas say, * It is right to respect the Smritis, because they are delivered by Vedic authors, because their origin is well established, and because they derive their authority from the Yeda, if but rightly understood.' The Munis see the Sruti, and they deliver the Smriti ; therefore the authority of both is proved on earth by full evidence. A man who despises these two, and adopts fallacious doctrines, is to be avoided by good men as a heretic and Veda-blasphemer.

" But one might object that if these precepts can be learnt from the Sruti, the Smriti would be unnecessary, because that only which cannot be learnt from other sources forms a fit object for a new work. Here then we say that these precepts, though they can be learnt from the Veda, are nevertheless put together in the Smritis for the purpose of making the order of their performance more easy, by leaving out the Arthavadas, and by taking from some &akhas of the Veda particular facts omitted in others. Now it might again be objected that this 1 Taittiriya-aranyaka, ii. 10.

is done in the Kalpa-sutras, and that therefore there was no necessity for the Smritis. But this is wrong, because there are two different kinds of duties to be performed, called Srauta (based on Sruti) and Smdrta (based on Smriti). The Srauta ceremonies consist in sacrifices like the Darsa-purnamasa, &c, which can only be performed after the sacred fire has been placed in the house, and they are clearly based upon the Yeda, as we read it. The Smdrta sacraments and traditional customs, on the contrary, consist in ablutions, rinsing the mouth, &c, and they are to be considered as based upon a Sakha of the Yeda which is hidden, but the existence of which must be inferred. Although, therefore, those precepts which regard the placing of the sacred fire, &c, are put together in the Kalpa-sutras, yet as other duties, such as ablutions, rinsing, &c, are not included in them, the Smritis have still their legitimate object."

This discussion has been given here at full length because it is a genuine specimen of Indian orthodox dialectics. Whatever may be thought of this style of argument, we see at all events how great an importance has always been attached by the Brahmans to the distinction between Sruti and Smriti.

It may also have been observed in this extract, that it is not quite in accordance with the language of Sayana to speak of Sutra works as Smritis in the plural. He applies this term to metrical codes only, like Manu, Yajnavalkya, and Parasara, but not to Sutras or Yedangas.1 This, however, does not affect 1 Kumarila remarks that, although the six Vedangas are not called by the name of Smriti, they are Smriti in the same sense SUTRAS EXCLUDED FROM THE SRUTI. 95 our present question, because even Sayana, though he does not call the Sutras by the name of Smritis, places them notwithstanding in the same category with the codes of law, and separates them from the Sruti, upon which they are founded, but with which they are not to be confounded. The Kalpa-sutras are called by him srauta, i. e. based on revelation, but not Sruti (revelation), because although they treat of the same subjects as the Sruti, they are themselves extracts only from the sacred writings. They are arranged by authors whose names are given, while, according to Indian notions, Mantras and Brahmanas were only seen by the Rishis, but neither composed nor arranged by them.1 That Sutras, even where they contain Yedangadoctrines, are distinctly excluded from the Sruti, may be seen from the following passage. In the Tantravarttika (1. 3.), Kumarila says, "There is a great as the Dharma-sutras, i. 3. 9. ^frJH «Bi'IT*lf ^IT^^TWf ^Tf%fl[^ll *T?lfa wf?nr%T *l HIM I *rfa$^fTTI ^J^' WT cf ^^^3{^n^n^f^TTf%^Tll Mahadeva, in his Commentary on the Hiranyakesi-sutras, says distinctly, f^^^ WT^T^ 1 " When we spoke of this (the authorship of Madhuchhandas) to a learned Hindu friend, he exhibited very marked dissatisfaction and distress,, begging us to write and tell Professor Wilson that the hymn had no author; that it had existed from everlasting ; and that Madhuchhandas was only the fortunate seer to whom, on the last occasion of its revelation, it had been revealed."— Benares Magazine for June 1851, i( On Miiller's Edition and Wilson's Version of the Risr-Veda."

difference between the Kalpa-sutras, which teach the performance of sacrifices enjoined by the Yedas, such as we now possess them, and the Smritis, which derive their authority from parts of the Veda that have either disappeared or are dispersed, or the existence of which can be proved by induction only. It is easier, therefore, to establish the authority of the Kalpa-sutras than that of the Smritis. The objections which have been raised against the authority of the Smritis, and which had to be removed by us before, cannot be thought of with regard to the Kalpa-sutras, not even if it were only for argument's sake.1 The question, therefore, is only this, whether the Kalpa-sutras have the same authority as the Veda, or whether they merely derive their authority from it. As the Yeda is called 4 shadanga,' i having six members/ these six members, and amongst them the Kalpa-sutras, might seem to be implied by the common name of Veda. This, however, would be wrong2; for the Kalpa-sutras, as is well-known, are composed by human authors like Masaka, &c. They do not take their names, like the Kathaka and other Sakhas of the Yeda, from those by whom they were proclaimed, but from their real authors. It is truer no doubt, that the authors of the Kalpa-sutras have the name of Rishis, and it might be said that as Sisu ^ Uc2TfTT*pi ST* Tp^m ^f%WTII sftTKAS EXCLUDED FROM THE SRUTI. 97 Angirasa was not the author of the Saisava hymns in the Samaveda, the Kalpa-sutras too were not composed, but only proclaimed, by those whose names they bear, particularly as there are even Brahmanas, for instance those of the Aruna and Parasara-sakha, which have nearly the same form as the Kalpa-sutras. Nevertheless, nothing can be more mistaken than the opinion of those who would put the Kalpa-sutras on the same footing as the Yeda, because people who teach and learn the Kalpa-sutras know that there was a time when these works did not exist, and that they were composed by certain authors like Masaka, Baudhayana, Apastamba, Asvalayana, Katyayana, and others."1 They are drawn, as he 1 Kumarila expressly observes that these names signify certain individuals, and not Charanas (sects), like those of Katha, by which certain &akhas of the Veda were promulgated.

u The branches of the Veda which were proclaimed by the sects of Katha and others from all eternity, have a fair claim to be called eternal. But this does not apply to works handed down by the sects or families of Masaka and others, however lono* they may have been established. For names like Masaka, Baudhayana, and Apastamba, imply an individual being which had a beginning, and therefore it is impossible that a title derived from these names should ever belong to an eternal work." And again :

observes in another place, partly from the Veda, but partly also from other sources ; and the same applies, according to him, to all the Vedangas and Smritis ; nay, even to later works, such as the epic and pauranic poems.1 " For teachers and pupils do not only know by heart the Kalpasutra books, and the other Vedanga and Smriti compositions, but they also remember Asvalayana, Baudhayana, Apastamba, Katyayana, and others, as the authors of these books."

rprf *rf?r:ii M All that has reference to virtue and final beatitude is taken from the Veda, while other matters3 the purpose of which consists in pleasure and gain, are according to the customs of men.
This distinction applies not only to the Vedangas, but also to authoritative passages in the Puranas and Itihasas."

Uvata, in his commentary on the 6akala pratisakya, takes the same view. He says, u that as the Veda was too difficult to be used by itself, learned men have extracted from it different doctrines on the ceremonial, the metre, and grammar, and brought them into a more intelligible form in the Sutras."
^TWT f^Ef5T 3f*frs? WT^Hft"*HMl*ilf% f^SJTOT- And again :

SRAUTA- ASD SMARTA-sti'IRAS. 99 It might therefore be best to distinguish between Smriti or tradition in general, and the Smritis or lawbooks in particular. We might then speak of srautaand swarfa-sutras, comprehending by the former name all Sutras, the source of which can be traced in the Sruti ; by the latter those of which no such source exists, or at least, is known to exist.1 The title of Smritis in the plural (or Smriti-prabandhas) might be left, for convenience sake, to such works as Sayana is speaking of, which are composed not in Sutras but in Slokas. It ought to be remembered, however, that the same subjects which are treated in the metrical Smritis of Manu and others, had similarly been treated in Sutras (srauta, grihya, and s&mayacharika), and that the principal difference between the two lies, not in their matter, but in their age, and their style.

1 Thus, smdrtam karma is well defined by Shadgurusishya in the Sarvanukramanibhashya, as 'nishekadi smasanantam smritigrihyavihitam karma.' In the Commentary on Asvalayana's 6rauta-sutras, it is said, that, if observances, like rinsing the mouth, &c., are prescribed in the 6rauta-sutras (as they are for instance Asval. i. 1. 3.), this is only done in order to show that such observances are acknowledged and presupposed by the Srautasutras, though they belong to the province of the Grihya ceremonies.

An objection against this division and terminology, not unknown to the Brahraans themselves, is that it is difficult to say whether certain Smarta-sutras may not be based upon some lost S&kha of the Yeda. The Srauta portions of the Kalpa-sutras, there can be no doubt, are founded on Sruti, if by this name we understand not only the hymns, but also the Brahmanas of the Yeda. But there are only few allusions, even in the Brahmanas, to the ceremonies described in the Grihya-sutras ; and the few passages which are quoted from the Sruti in their support, are chiefly taken from the Aranyakas and Upanishads, the latest branches of Yedic literature. As to the Ach&ras, or the established rules of conduct with regard to particular temporal duties, even Indian writers admit that there are only very vague allusions to them in the Sruti, and they try to prove that these laws are based on parts of the Yeda which no longer exist. This is a view which is taken for instance by Haradatta in his Commentary on Apastamba's Samayach&rikasutras, and it deserves to be examined more closely. On the first Sutra1, " Therefore let us now explain the Sdmaydchdrika duties" he makes the following observations.

" The word c therefore1 implies a reason, which is that as the srauta (sacrificial) and gdrhya (domestic) ceremonies have been explained, and as these ceremonies presuppose other observances, these other observances must now be explained too. For when it was said before (in the iSrauta and Grihya-sutras), that such and such an act was to be performed by a man after 1 ^wr: *™ ^ i ^ i ft^T^^T^T w^t*t: 11 \ w LOST SAKIIAS. 101 he had rinsed his mouth, by a man who is clean, who holds a pavitra in his hand, who is invested with the sacred thread, &c, an acquaintance with all these things, such as rinsing, &c, is presupposed. The twilight prayers, too, are referred to in the preceding Sutras, when it is said, that a man who does not perform his twilight prayers is impure, and unworthy of every sacrifice. Several other instances occur; and it is therefore necessary to explain now immediately those other precepts called samayacharika (temporal). Sdmaydchdrika is derived from samaya (agreement) and d'chdra (custom). Samaya, a human agreement, is of three kinds : vidhi, injunction ; niyama, restriction ; pratishedha, prohibition. Rules founded upon samaya are called samaydchdras, from which the adjective samayacharika. Dharma (virtue) is the quality of the individual self, which arises from action, leads to happiness and final beatitude, and is called apiwva, supernatural. But, in our Sutra, dharma means law, and has for its object dharma as well as adharma: things to be done and things to be avoided.
zh: 吠陀傳誦文獻（*smriti*）與吠陀啟示文獻（*sruti*）的區別，乃是印度宗教史中最重要的區分之一。啟示文獻被視為直接源於神聖之源，因而具有絕對權威；而傳誦文獻則被理解為人類對啟示文獻的記憶與闡釋，其權威性因此為次級性質。 傳誦文獻包括法典、史詩及其他著作，其內容雖源自吠陀的精神，卻是由人類聖賢與立法者編纂而成。這些文獻在印度宗教與法律傳統中佔據重要地位，為後世信徒提供實踐指引。然而，由於其人為性質，傳誦文獻被允許隨時代而演變與修訂，以適應不同時期與地域的需求。 此區別反映了印度宗教思想中關於啟示與理性、神聖與人為之間的根本關係。傳誦文獻的彈性與可變性，使其能夠將永恆的吠陀智慧應用於人間事務，從而維繫了吠陀傳統與現實生活之間的活力關聯。
婆羅門人以此論證來證實其法律文獻的權威性。

「難道看起來不是這樣嗎，」他說，「這部《法經》（Smriti，包含人類的法律在內）似乎根本不值得有自己的註疏，因為很難看出它憑什麼基礎來聲稱任何權威性？蓋若我們訴諸於耆那教（Jaimini，《前彌曼差》Purva-miinansa 的創始人）的一部經（Sutra），其中他已證明吠陀（Veda）擁有不依賴於任何他物的權威性，那麼這些論證幾乎不可能適用於明顯是人類著作、且完全依賴於其來源權威性的著作。這些來源本身，若被視為該權威性的生命和力量所在，往往極其模糊不清。首先，它們根本不可能落在感官的認知範圍內，因為義務或法律的本質本身是超越性的。也不能在歸納法中尋獲這一終極理由或來源，蓋歸納法只有在觀察之後才可能。同樣不能在他人的言論中尋求，因為人類容易犯錯，甚至不能按照他真實感知的方式表達事物。但即使人類沒有錯誤，仍然總會有懷疑和反對的餘地。至於在《聖論》（Sruti，即《頌文》Mantras 和《梵書》Brahmanas）的直接教誨中尋獲這些法律的權威性，那是不可能的，因為這樣的教誨根本不存在於其中。我們從未在吠陀中看到過類似《法經》那樣的教誨，如保持身體潔淨等等。承認這類法律的歸納法權利將是極其危險的，因為它將同樣地適用於佛陀的教誨、對聖墓的崇拜等等。然而，對所有這些疑惑都有一個答案。」
《摩奴智經》與《佛陀智經》之間存在著巨大的差異，因為摩奴的權威乃由無可否認的《吠陀》本身所主張。《吠陀》中有言：「摩奴所言，悉為妙藥；」然而其中並無任何段落以任何方式支持《佛陀智經》，因此應用歸納法的權利不能視為危險，蓋其決不可能延伸至佛陀的教義。

反對者說：「不是這樣。」「無論摩奴所說的，皆為藥物」這則吠陀經文，僅是一條經義疏釋（arthavada，即解釋性的評論），本身並無證據。它只是用來闡明或推薦另一條戒律，即摩奴的兩首詩頌應在某一特定祭祀中誦用。¹因此，吠陀中並無任何經文足以保證《智度論》（Smriti）的權威性；若釋迦牟尼，即佛陀的《智度論》有可非難之處，則摩奴的《智度論》亦然適用。因此有言：「因為人們常說謊言且易於犯錯，又無神聖戒律賦予，故唯有信仰才能成為權威。」然而¹於蘇摩祭儀中作為 *dhayyas*，置於《燧火讚歌集》（Samidheni）的中間。同樣的論證出現在庫瑪里羅的《譚特羅注疏》（Tantra-varttika）第一卷第三節。摩訶提婆在其《褐鼎濕奴經軌注疏》（Commentary on the Hiranyakesi-sutras）中說，聲聞經（*sruti*）乃以「無論摩奴所說的，皆為藥物」之言，來證明《智度論》的權威性。
再者，即便承認摩奴（Manu）有某種權威的影子存在，又該如何為帕剎婆（Parasara）的《法經》（Smriti）辯護呢？因為雖然《吠陀》可能稱頌摩奴，卻從未如此對待帕剎婆，因此帕剎婆的權威至少很難得到辯護。

對此我們的回答是：《法經》（Smritis）具有權威性，因為它們應當具有權威性是不言自明的；而且沒有任何理由不把它們視為具有權威性。有人提出三個理由說明摩奴（Manu）及其他聖賢不能宣稱權威，即：一、人會說謊，二、人易於犯錯，三、沒有神聖的命令。然而這些反對意見完全不成立，因為沒有人會認為從出生起就完美的摩奴和帕剎舍羅（Parasara）會說謊或犯錯。就前兩個反對意見而言，姑且不論。那麼，誰曾否認過這些聖賢從出生起就是完美的呢？《咒句經》（Mantras）、《義疏》（Arthavadas）、《歷史詩》（Itihasas）和《往世書》（Puranas）清晰地證明了不僅帕剎舍羅等人的存在，而且證明了他們的完美性。誠然，即便我們沒有《咒句經》的見證，帕剎舍羅等人的完美性——這完美性包含於他們的存在本身——又怎能被否認呢？意見分歧是絕不可能的。而且在《烏多耶彌彌數論》（Uttara-mimansa）中論及神祇的篇章裡，是否已經證明了《咒句經》不需要

任何進一步的證據來支持他們所說的話？誠然，在論述《阿闍婆陀》（Arthavadas）的章節中，已經承認《阿闍婆陀》所包含的內容並不總是值得相信的。但這僅僅是因為其中偶爾會提到某些不可能的事物。因此，像「無論摩奴說什麼都是藥」（'Whatever Manu says is medicine'）這樣的《阿闍婆陀》，雖然它只是為了推薦另一條律例，但本身仍必須被視為真實的。關於釋迦，無法提出任何類似的論點；因此，在別處說得很好：「願人蔑視阿羅漢、遮羅迦和佛陀的一切謬誤教義。」（'May a man scorn all the erroneous doctrines of Arhat, Chārvāka, and Buddha.'）至於帕拉沙羅特別是，說他的名望並不同樣建立在《吠陀》之上，這是錯誤的，因為《斯魯提》中說：「如此言帕拉沙羅之子毗耶娑。」（'Thus spoke Vyasa, the son of Parasara'）因此，如果著名的吠陀‧毗耶娑被讚美為帕拉沙羅之子，那麼他的父親帕拉沙羅的名望豈不更加卓著！在瑜伽娑尼耶支派的系譜婆羅門經中，帕拉沙羅的兒子和孫子也被提及於傳承《吠陀》的師生相承的序列中：「葛黎陀考希卡受教於帕拉沙羅耶那，帕拉沙羅耶那受教於帕拉沙羅耶，帕拉沙羅耶受教於雅圖迦爾尼耶，等等。」因此帕拉沙羅完全與摩奴相提並論；同樣適用於所有其他仙人，如跋濕笈多和耶若那瓦爾基亞，他們是《往世書》的作者，且在《斯魯提》中被提及。我們就這樣讀到：「眾仙人並未清楚地看見因陀羅，但跋濕笈多清楚地看見了他。」（'The Rishis did not see Indra clearly, but Vasishtha saw him clearly.'）¹ ¹ 《大森林奧義書》，5. 6. 3.
2 《泰提利耶本集》(Taittiriya-Samhita)，3. 5. 2。
4 阿特里將其子女交給渴望兒子的奧爾瓦。7 * l 耶奴瓦爾基亞有兩位妻子。2 因此，無論如何都不應當企圖以任何手段攻擊《摩奴法典》（Smritis of Manu）及其他經典。

第三個反對《往世書》（Smriti）權威性的理由，即聖仙們並未清晰地見到因陀羅，然而婆私多卻清晰地見到了他。因陀羅說：「我將告訴你一部《婆羅門書》（Brahmana），好使所有出生的人都以你為淨火祭司（Purohita）；但不要把關於我的事告訴其他聖仙。」因此他將讚歌的這些部分傳授給他；自此以後，人們出生時便以婆私多為淨火祭司。因此應當選擇一位婆私多作為婆羅門祭司。」參見《坦迪耶婆羅門書》（Tandya Brahmana）卷十五·第五節，其中論及婆羅闍人（Bharata）將永遠以婆私多為淨火祭司。該處註釋者指出，婆羅闍（Bharata）既可指同名的國王，亦可泛指一般的人類。
塔帝利亞梵經集 7. 1. 8.

我注意到您提供的英文原文中包含大量亂碼字符，使得內容無法正確識別。可辨識的部分為： > "Atri gave his children to the son of Urva, who longed for a son. Then he felt lonely, and saw that he was without power, weak, and decrepit. He saw this Chatxiratra; he took it and sacrificed with it. Four sons were born to him from it, — a good Hotri, a good Udgatri, a good Adhvaryu, and a good Brahman." 以下為此段的翻譯： 「阿特里（Atri）將他的孩子們賜予烏爾瓦（Urva）之子，他渴望得到一個兒子。後來他感到寂寞，看到自己沒有力量，衰弱且蒼老。他看到這個查圖拉拉（Chatxiratra）；他取得它並以它進行祭祀。四個兒子由此而生——一位優秀的霍特里（Hotri）、一位優秀的烏德加特里（Udgatri）、一位優秀的阿德瓦尤（Adhvaryu），以及一位優秀的婆羅門（Brahman）。」 **建議**：請重新提供清晰的英文原文，以便進行準確的翻譯。
2 《百道梵書》（Çatapatha-brahmana），第十七卷，4‧5

93 所有在《頌文》中給予的教訓並非基於《吠陀聖言》的段落，此說不符事實。因為我們確實遇到了那些是《頌文》中所有律法之源的段落。因此我們讀到：「這五大祭祀每日開始，每日舉行：對諸神的祭祀（Devayajna）、對先祖亡靈的祭祀（Pitriyajna）、對一切眾生的祭祀（Bhutayajna）、對人類的祭祀（Manushyayajna）、對梵我的祭祀（Brahmayajna）。」儘管《吠陀》中對於沐浴等並無明確的教訓，然而所有這些皆已暗示其中。因此跋陀遮耶們說：「尊重《頌文》是正當的，因為它們是由吠陀作者所傳述，因為其起源確立無疑，因為它們若得到正確的理解，其權威源自《吠陀》。」諸仙人洞見《吠陀聖言》，而他們傳述《頌文》；因此兩者的權威在人間皆以完全的證據而得以確立。一個鄙視這兩者而採納謬誤學說的人，應被善良的人視為異端邪說者與褻瀆吠陀者而加以迴避。

但有人可能會提出異議，若這些戒律可以從《吠陀》（Sruti）習得，則《法經》（Smriti）便無必要，因為只有那些無法從其他來源習得的內容才是著述新作的恰當對象。對此我們回答說，這些戒律雖然可以從《吠陀》習得，然而之所以在《法經》中將其匯編在一起，乃是為了便於其施行之序次，藉由刪除《義疏》（Arthavadas），以及取自某些《吠陀》分支（śākhās）中在其他分支所遺漏的特定內容。然而，人們可能再次提出異議，即¹ ¹ 《泰提利耶林書》（Taittiriya-aranyaka），二、10。

是在《迦爾帕蘇特拉》（Kalpa-sutras）中進行的，因此就沒有必要編訂《誦傳法典》了。但這是錯誤的，因為有兩種不同類型的義務需要履行，分別稱為《誦經義務》（Srauta，基於《誦經》）和《傳統義務》（Smṛta，基於《誦傳法典》）。《誦經義務》的儀式包括像《新月滿月祭》（Darśa-pūrṇamāsa）等獻祭，這些只能在聖火已被安置於家中之後才能進行，它們明顯是以我們所讀到的吠陀為基礎的。相反地，《傳統義務》的聖禮和傳統習俗包括淨身、漱口等，應當被視為基於吠陀的某個分支（Śākhā）——這個分支是隱沒的，但其存在必須透過推理而得出。因此，儘管那些涉及聖火安置等的規則已被彙編在《迦爾帕蘇特拉》中，然而由於其他義務（如淨身、漱口等）並未包含其中，《誦傳法典》仍然具有其正當的作用。
本段討論之所以詳細闡述於此，乃因其為印度正統辯證法的真正範例。無論對此論證風格有何看法，我們無論如何都可以看到，婆羅門人對於《斯魯提》（Sruti）與《斯姆利提》（Smriti）之間的區別一向賦予多大的重要性。

從此節錄中也可以觀察到，用「頌文」（Smritis）的複數形式來談論《蘇特羅》（Sutra）著作並不完全符合沙耶那（Sayana）的用語習慣。他僅將此術語應用於韻文法典，如《摩奴法典》、《耶若尼亞瓦爾克亞法典》和《帕拉沙羅法典》，而非應用於《蘇特羅》或《吠陀各分科》（Yedangas）。¹ 然而這並不影響我們目前的論證，因為即使沙耶那並未以「頌文」之名稱呼《蘇特羅》，他仍將其與法律法典歸為同一類別，並將其從其所依據的《聲聞》（Sruti）中區分開來，但不應將兩者混為一談。《卡爾帕蘇特羅》（Kalpa-sutras）被他稱為「源自啟示」（srauta），即立基於啟示，但非《聲聞》（啟示），因為雖然它們論述與《聲聞》相同的主題，但它們本身僅為聖典的摘錄而已。這些著作由有名字記載的作者編纂，而根據印度觀念，咒文（Mantras）和婆羅門書（Brahmanas）只是為聖仙（Rishis）所見聞，既非由他們撰述，亦非由他們編排。¹ 即使《蘇特羅》包含《吠陀各分科》的教義，其亦明確地被排除在《聲聞》之外，此可由下列段落看出。在《譚特羅瓦爾蒂卡》（Tantravarttika）（1.3.）中，庫瑪里拉（Kumarila）說：「《達摩蘇特羅》之與《聲聞》相較，差異甚大。」¹ 摩訶德瓦（Mahadeva）在其《希蘭亞克西蘇特羅》（Hiranyakesi-sutras）的注疏中明確指出：¹ 「當我們向一位博學的印度朋友談及此事（關於馬都闡達斯的著作權）時，他表現出深深的不滿和悲傷，懇求我們寫信告訴威爾遜教授，此讚歌並無著者；它自亙古即存在；而馬都闡達斯只是有幸的聖仙，在最後一次啟示的時刻，它對他顯現。」——《貝拿勒斯雜誌》1851年6月號，〈論穆勒的《梨俱吠陀》版本與威爾遜的譯本〉。

《羯磨經》（Kalpa-sutras）與《法經》（Smritis）之間的區別在於：前者教導《吠陀》所規定之祭祀的執行，如我們今日所擁有的那樣；後者的權威則來自《吠陀》中已然消失或散佚，或其存在只能通過歸納法證明的那些部分。因此，建立《羯磨經》的權威要比建立《法經》的權威來得容易。之前對《法經》權威所提出的異議，以及我們必須消除的那些異議，即使只為了論證起見，對於《羯磨經》也根本無須考慮。因此，問題僅在於此：《羯磨經》是否具有與《吠陀》相同的權威，或者它們是否只是從《吠陀》中衍生其權威。由於《吠陀》被稱為「沙達安加」（shadanga），即「具有六個分支」，這六個分支，其中包括《羯磨經》，似乎可能被視為包含在《吠陀》的共同名稱之中。然而，這種看法是錯誤的；因為《羯磨經》如眾所周知的那樣，是由馬薩迦（Masaka）等人類作者編撰的。它們的命名方式不同於《羯磨經》和《吠陀》的其他分支（Sakhas），這些分支是以傳述者的名字命名，而《羯磨經》是以其真實作者的名字命名的。誠然，《羯磨經》的作者確實有「仙人」（Rishis）的稱號，人們或許可以說，正如安吉拉薩（Angirasa）並非《沙摩吠陀》（Samaveda）中《塞沙瓦》讚歌的作者一樣，《羯磨經》也不是由以其名字命名的人所撰寫，而只是由他們所傳述的——尤其是因為甚至還存在著《婆羅門書》，例如阿魯那分支（Aruna-sakha）和帕剎沙羅分支（Parasara-sakha）的《婆羅門書》，其形式與《羯磨經》幾乎相同。然而，沒有什麼比以下觀點更加謬誤的了：那些希望將《羯磨經》置於與《吠陀》同等地位的人們的看法。因為教導和學習《羯磨經》的人們都知道，曾經有一段時期這些著作並不存在，它們是由馬薩迦、波陀亞那（Baudhayana）、阿帕斯坦巴（Apastamba）、阿瑞瓦利亞那（Asvalayana）、迦梯亞那（Katyayana）等特定作者所編撰的。它們被汲取自——如他所言——
「諸由迦打（Katha）等諸派世世代代宣揚之吠陀各分支，確實可稱之為永恆之物。然而此說不適用於摩娑迦（Masaka）等諸派或家族所傳承之著述，縱然其傳承之久遠。蓋如摩娑迦、跋陀衍那（Baudhayana）及阿跋斯檀跋（Apastamba）之名號，皆暗示有一受限於始終之個別存在，因此不可能有源自此等名號之冠名可以屬於永恆之著述。」復又云：

在另一處所觀察到的，一部分源自吠陀，但也部分源自其他資料；根據他的說法，同樣的情況也適用於所有的吠陀支（Vedangas）和傳誦書（Smritis）；甚至也適用於較晚的著作，如史詩和往世書（pauranic）詩篇。¹「教師和弟子們不僅能背誦《卡爾帕經》（Kalpasutra）書籍以及其他吠陀支和傳誦書的著作，而且他們也記得阿斯瓦拉亞那（Asvalayana）、鮑德哈亞那（Baudhayana）、阿帕斯坦巴（Apastamba）、迦堤亞那（Katyayana）及其他人，作為這些書籍的作者。」
凡與德行及最終極樂有關的一切，都取自《吠陀》，而其他事物——其目的在於快樂與利得者——則依人類的習俗而定。
這項區分不僅適用於吠陀附隨科學（Vedangas），也適用於《往世書》（Puranas）和《歷史敘事書》（Itihasas）中的權威性段落。
烏瓦塔（Uvata）在其《六本體護法經》（6akala pratisakya）的註疏中亦持同一觀點。他說：「由於吠陀過於深奧，不易自行運用，故學者從中萃取關於祭儀、韻律與文法的各種教說，並將其彙整為更易理解的蘇特拉形式。」
我已準備好進行翻譯，但我注意到您提供的「英文原文」部分包含亂碼（^TWT f^Ef5T 3f*frs? WT^Hft"*HMl*ilf% f^SJTOT）。 請提供清晰、完整的英文原文文本，我將按照您所述的規則進行專業翻譯。

因此最好還是區分斯瑪提（Smriti）即一般的傳統，與複數的斯瑪提經（Smritis）即特別的法典。我們可以區分斯勞塔經與斯瓦塔經（srauta- and swarta-sutras），前者涵括所有的經（Sutras），其源頭可溯自斯魯提（Sruti）；後者則是那些無此源頭，或至少尚不知道有此源頭的經。1 複數的斯瑪提經之名義（或稱斯瑪提文獻匯編 Smriti-prabandhas），為了方便起見，可保留用於薩亞那（Sayana）所談論的那類著作，此類著作非以經（Sutras）的形式成書，而是以詩偈（Slokas）的形式成書。然而應當記住，同樣的主題若在摩奴（Manu）等人的韻文斯瑪提經中有所論述，也同樣地在經（斯勞塔經、家禮經與世俗法經 srauta, grihya, and samayacharika）中得到處理，且兩者之間的主要差異不在其內容，而在於其時代與其文體。

因此，「記憶的業」（smdrtam karma）在沙德古魯希斯亞（Shadgurusishya）的《全部經義疏》（Sarvanukramanibhashya）中得到了明確的定義，即「從受孕開始至火葬結束，由《記憶經》（smriti）所規定的業」（nishekadi smasanantam smritigrihyavihitam karma）。在阿斯瓦拉亞那《聲聞經》（Asvaláyana's Srauta-sutras）的註疏中有云，若漱口等觀行被規定在《聲聞經》中（例如《阿斯瓦拉亞那經》第一卷第一篇第三節），其目的只在於表明此類觀行為《聲聞經》所承認與預設，雖然它們實屬於家庭祭祀（Grihya ceremonies）的範疇。

關於這個分類法和術語的反對意見，就連婆羅門人自身也不陌生，即難以判定某些《住宅經》（Smarta-sutras）是否可能奠基於某部已失傳的《吠陀》分支。《迦爾帕經》（Kalpa-sutras）中的《火祭部分》（Srauta portions）無疑是以《聲聞》（Sruti）為基礎，如果我們用此名稱不僅指吠陀讚歌，也指吠陀的《婆羅門書》（Brahmanas）。然而即便在《婆羅門書》中，對於《住家經》（Grihya-sutras）所述儀式的記載也寥寥無幾；而那些為其辯護而引用自《聲聞》的少數段落，主要取自《森林書》（Aranyakas）和《奧義書》（Upanishads），這乃吠陀文獻最晚近的分支。至於《行儀法》（Acharas），即關於特定時代義務的既定行為規則，甚至印度作家本身也承認在《聲聞》中對其的記載只是含糊其辭，他們試圖證明這些法則乃奠基於《吠陀》已不復存在的部分。例如哈剌達塔（Haradatta）在其《阿帕斯檀巴《時代行儀法經》註疏中便採取了這種觀點，此論點值得更深入的檢視。在第一條經文¹「因此讓我們現在闡述《時代行儀法義務》」上，他提出了以下看法。

「c」這個詞因此¹暗示了一個理由：既然斯勞塔（祭祀）與格爾海亞（家庭）儀式已經被解釋過，而這些儀式又預設了其他的遵行，那麼這些其他的遵行現在也必須被解釋。蓋當之前（在《斯勞塔經》與《格爾海亞經》中）說某某行為必須由一個人在漱口之後、由一個潔淨的人、手持淨火枝的人、配戴聖線的人等等來執行時，人們就已預設了對漱口等所有這些事物的認識。在先前的經文中也引及晨昏祈禱，說一個不進行晨昏祈禱的人是不潔的，不配進行任何祭祀。還有許多其他的例子；因此現在必須立即解釋那些稱為薩摩耶查里卡（時間性的）的其他戒律。薩摩耶查里卡一詞衍生自薩摩耶（協議）與阿查拉（習俗）。薩摩耶是人為的協議，分為三類：毘地（injunction，規範）、尼耶摩（niyama，限制）、普拉蒂謝德（pratishedha，禁止）。基於薩摩耶而制定的規則稱為薩摩耶查拉（samaydchdras），由此派生出形容詞薩摩耶查里卡。達摩（dharma，德行）是個人自我的一種特性，它源於行為，導向幸福與最終的解脫，被稱為阿普爾瓦（apiwva，超越的）。但在我們的經文中，達摩意指法則，其對象兼涵達摩與阿達摩：應該做的事與應該避免的事。
